title: The plugin that fixed my Claude Code workflow

Presenter frames it as ideal for complex app or automation builds where Claude Code needs structured guidance to avoid missing steps. Not designed for quick tasks. Link to repo in comments.

title: Use these 3 tricks to reduce your Claude code token usage.

eval.summary: Short video covering three token-reduction techniques for Claude Code: (1) Use /model to assign Opus for planning and Sonnet for execution, since Opus burns tokens faster but Sonnet handles most coding tasks adequately. (2) Use /compact when conversations grow long — Claude re-reads full history on every message, so compacting summarizes context to reduce token load while preserving key info. (3) Prepend 'ultra think' to prompts before complex tasks to force full problem reasoning before code generation. Video is a teaser for a paid 3-hour Claude Code course.
techniques_mentioned: model tiering for planning vs execution

patterns.description: Tier models by task phase: use a stronger model (Opus) for planning/architecture and a cheaper model (Sonnet) for execution/coding — reducing token cost without sacrificing output quality
patterns.apply_to: orchestrator.py task routing — assign planning subtasks to higher capability tier, execution subtasks to Sonnet; document in CLAUDE.md as default agent behavior

patterns.description: Context compaction strategy: summarize/compress conversation history when context window fills rather than truncating or starting fresh — preserves continuity while reducing token burn
patterns.apply_to: session-log.md protocol and orchestrator context management — implement a compaction step when session token count crosses a threshold before spawning new agent calls

patterns.description: 'Ultra think' prefix forces extended reasoning before code generation — analogous to explicit chain-of-thought priming that improves plan quality on complex tasks
patterns.apply_to: CLAUDE.md system prompt design and orchestrator prompt templates — add a reasoning-first instruction to complex task prompts, especially for TradeOS signal logic or multi-step agent workflows

patterns.description: Claude re-reads entire conversation history on every message — this is a token architecture fact that should inform how we structure long-running agent sessions (short sessions, frequent summarization, avoid padding context with irrelevant tool outputs)
patterns.apply_to: orchestrator_v2.py session design — keep agent message threads short, summarize tool outputs before appending to context, avoid logging raw verbose outputs into active context

stack_evaluation.replaces_or_complements: pattern only — no tool; complements existing Claude API usage and orchestrator.py prompt design
stack_evaluation.reasoning: Lens 1 drives this verdict. All three techniques are directly applicable to our stack with minimal effort. Model tiering and context compaction are immediately actionable in orchestrator prompt templates and session management. The 'ultra think' prefix is a zero-cost improvement to complex task prompts.

strategic_signal.signal: Token cost management is becoming a primary UX pain point for Claude Code power users — Veritas should consider surfacing token usage telemetry and auto-compaction logic as a differentiating feature in AgentOS, especially as parallel batch workloads scale.

title: Claude Code skill-building tips shift #claudecode #skillbuilding #aiworkflows

eval.summary: A Claude Code engineer shares three skill-building tips: (1) Add a 'gotchas' section to each skill file — a running list of mistakes Claude has made that you don't want repeated, which acts as a feedback loop for improvement over time. (2) Structure skills as folders, not single markdown files — include scripts, references, examples, and templates alongside the prompt; Claude pulls these in only as needed, avoiding context window bloat. (3) Avoid over-constraining Claude — give context and constraints but leave room for Claude to adapt when invoking a skill, as overly rigid instructions cause Claude to struggle. These are framed as optimizations for Claude Code workflows specifically.

patterns.description: Structure skills as folders rather than single markdown files — co-locate the prompt with scripts, templates, reference docs, and examples so Claude pulls them in on demand rather than loading everything into context
patterns.apply_to: Skill architecture under .lux/ or skills/ directory — replace flat .md skill files with named folders containing prompt.md, scripts/, examples/, templates/ subdirectories

patterns.description: Use progressive disclosure: Claude references companion files only as needed rather than having all content embedded in the main prompt, reducing context window bloat on skill invocation

patterns.apply_to: Skill prompt authoring convention — audit existing skill files for over-railroading; replace procedural chains with intent + constraints + examples format

title: AI TOOLS TIER LIST (2026)

eval.summary: Dan Martell runs a quick tier list of AI tools for 2026. ChatGPT gets D, Claude Chat gets A (slow sometimes), NotebookLM gets A, Grok gets B, Gemini gets A (valued for YouTube/Google Docs search), Perplexity gets low B (only for new computer feature), Whisper Flow gets A (daily use for voice-to-structured-text), Apple Intelligence gets E---. Claude Code gets top S tier — 'you can build every product with a prompt that sounds like English.' OpenClaude (Claude CLI wrapper) gets S+++ — Martell uses it daily and is building a company called Apex around it. Core signal: Claude Code + Claude CLI combination framed as the agentic build stack of choice for operators in 2026.
